coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon quantum dots/TiO2 hybrid composites with enhanced visible light driven photocatalytic activity toward dye wastewater degradation and mechanism insight
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jun
Zhang, Xueying
Dong, Shanshan
Zhou, Xian
Dong, Shuangshi
coredata.dc:description: N-doped carbon quantum dots/TiO2 (NCQDs/TiO2) hybrid composites were prepared via a simple and efficient low-temperature process. The resulted composites were characterized by various techniques, such as X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, scanning electron microscope, transmission electron microscopy and high-resolution TEM, and UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra. The results indicated that the NCQDs were successfully introduced onto the TiO2 surface. The photocatalytic activity of the NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites were investigated by using Methylene blue (MB) as the target pollutant. The photodegradation efficiency of MB over 1NCQDs/TiO2 was 86.9% within 420min, which was much higher than those of pristine TiO2 (53.8%) and the hybrid composites with other NCQDs contents (lower than 80.1%). This suggested that the NCQDs played important roles for MB degradation under visible light irradiation. The functions of NCQDs were discussed and a new synergistic mechanism was proposed for the degradation of MB on the surface of NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites. The enhanced activities were attributed to the interfacial transfer of photogenerated charges from the conductive band (CB) of TiO2 to NCQDs, leading to effective charge separation of TiO2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
NCQDs/TiO2
Photocatalytic activity
Visible light irradiation

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors greatly thank the State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology (QA201418), the Science and Technology Development Program of Jilin province, China (20140101159JC) and Graduate Innovation Fund of Jilin University (2015057) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorption (ABS) spectra of the CQDs and NCQDs. Insets shows photographs of CQDs and NCQDs solutions under UV light (365nm).
XRD patterns of NCQDs/TiO2 composites with different amount of NCQDs.
XPS spectra of 1NCQDs/TiO2 composites for (a) Full spectra; (b) Ti 2p; (c) C 1s; (d) O 1s; (e)N 1s.
SEM image (a) and EDS analysis (b) of the 1NCQDs/TiO2. (c) TEM and (d) HRTEM images of the 1NCQDs/TiO2.
FT-IR spectra of NCQDs/TiO2 composites with different amount of NCQDs.
UV–vis DRS spectra of NCQDs/TiO2 composites with different amount of NCQDs.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of MB solution in the presence of pristine TiO2 and NCQDs/TiO2 with different amount of NCQDs under visible light irradiation. (b) Recyclability of the photocatalytic decomposition of MB by 1NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composite.

The important roles of NCQDs for enhancing the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 with the separation and transfer of photogenerated charges in the NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites under the visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon quantum dots/TiO2 hybrid composites with enhanced visible light driven photocatalytic activity toward dye wastewater degradation and mechanism insight

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources and Environment, Ministry of Education, Jilin University, Changchun, 130021, Jilin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources and Environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N-doped carbon quantum dots/TiO2 (NCQDs/TiO2) hybrid composites were prepared via a simple and efficient low-temperature process. The resulted composites were characterized by various techniques, such as X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, scanning electron microscope, transmission electron microscopy and high-resolution TEM, and UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra. The results indicated that the NCQDs were successfully introduced onto the TiO2 surface. The photocatalytic activity of the NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites were investigated by using Methylene blue (MB) as the target pollutant. The photodegradation efficiency of MB over 1NCQDs/TiO2 was 86.9% within 420min, which was much higher than those of pristine TiO2 (53.8%) and the hybrid composites with other NCQDs contents (lower than 80.1%). This suggested that the NCQDs played important roles for MB degradation under visible light irradiation. The functions of NCQDs were discussed and a new synergistic mechanism was proposed for the degradation of MB on the surface of NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites. The enhanced activities were attributed to the interfacial transfer of photogenerated charges from the conductive band (CB) of TiO2 to NCQDs, leading to effective charge separation of TiO2.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor-based photocatalysis has attracted massive attention, since it could remove environmental pollutants and generate hydrogen from splitting water using solar energy [1–3]. In order to effectively harness abundant sunlight, the visible light driven photocatalyst is necessary to be successfully developed. To date, TiO2 is considered as one of the most promising photocatalysts due to its good chemical stability, photocatalytic activity and environmental friendly behavior [3,4]. Unfortunately, the relatively wide band gap of TiO2 (3.0–3.2eV) greatly limited its application, because it is activated solely by UV light with a wave length of no longer than 387.5nm [5,6]. However, sunlight comprises 3–5% ultraviolet light [7–9] and 42–45% visible light, numerous efforts have been done on developing photocatalysts that are effective in visible light region [10]. Various methods, such as non-metal doping, noble metal deposition, and coupling with semiconductors, have been applied [11–13]. Meanwhile, the dye photosensitization is also widely investigated. In a typical process of dye photosensitization degradation, a dye molecule can be absorbed on the surface of TiO2, which is the prerequisite for photosensitization. Then, the dye molecule is excited by and the excited electrons would inject into the conduction band (CB) of TiO2 and further react with O2 to generate O2 − to further oxidize pollutants [14–16]. Thus, a suitable dye could be degraded under visible light irradiation through photosensitization effect.

Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new member of carbon family with sizes below 10nm, have gradually become a rising star due to their unique properties including large two-photon absorption cross section, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility [17–19]. The conjugated structure of CQDs leads to the excellent electron transfer/reservoir properties, which is the key factor in enhancing the photocatalytic activity. The synthesis of composites with combination CQDs and semiconductor (such as TiO2, ZnO, Cu2O, BiVO4, BiWO4 and g-C3N4) and their enhanced visible light photocatalytic activity have been studied [9,10,20–24]. Recently, it is reported that N-doped nanostructured carbon materials can efficiently induce charge delocalization, lower the work function of carbon, effectively enhance the photoluminescence emission performance and give access to tunable electronic and optical properties of CQDs [25–27]. The N doping can induce new unique physical and chemical properties of carbon. The effective NCQDs/rutile TiO2 composites were firstly synthesized by Zhang et al. [9]. In their work, the as-synthesized NCQDs/TiO2 composites showed an enhanced photocatalytic activity, compared with single CQDs, NCQDs and TiO2. Compared with N-free CQDs, NCQDs showed enhanced photocatalytic activity and better DSSCs performance when they were applied in TiO2-based composites photocatalysts and DSSCs, which was attributed to their lower work function induced by N doping. According to other researchers [20,21], there is a huge impact on the photocatalytic efficiency of CQDs/semiconductor caused by the amount of CQDs. However, the effect of NCQDs/TiO2 ratio on its photocatalytic activity has never been reported until now. Moreover, low-cost and abundant starting materials such as citric acid solid has been proved to be the carbon source of CQDs which possessed have rich hydroxyl [28].

In this paper, NCQDs/TiO2 composites photocatalysts were prepared through a low-temperature method. The as-obtained NCQDs/TiO2 exhibited obviously superior photocatalytic activity than TiO2 under visible light irradiation. A new synergistic photosensitized mechanism was proposed. The important roles of NCQDs and structure of TiO2 in NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites in the enhancement of photocatalytic activity was discussed.

TiO2 nanoparticle was prepared by a hydrothermal method according to our previous report [29,30], without addition of Er3+:YAlO3. NCQDs solution was synthesized by the one-step of hydrothermal treatment as follows. First, 0.42g citric acid solid was dissolved in 10mL deionized water, with addition of 0.536mL ethylenediamine (C2H8N2) into the mixed solution. Then, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. After that, the autoclave was heated at a constant temperature of 200°C for 5h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the NCQDs solution was centrifuged to remove weight precipitate and any impurities. The synthesis of CQDs was similar to that of NCQDs except that C2H8N2 was not used. In order to be used easily in subsequent experiments, the NCQDs and CQDs solution have been diluted 100 times with deionized water.

In a typical synthesis procedure of NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites, 0.1g of TiO2 were ultrasonically dispersed in x (x=0.2, 1, 2, 4 or 10) mL of NCQDs solution. The mixed solution dried at 80°C overnight, the products were washed with distilled water and absolute ethanol for several times and then dried at 80°C for further characterizations. The hybrid composites were denoted as xNCQDs/TiO2.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were performed by a Bruker D8 Advanced X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (λ=0.15418nm) at a scanning rate of 3°/min in the range of 2θ from 10° to 80°.The surface chemical bonding states of the photocatalysts were analyzed by performing X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on a Kratos Axis Ultra spectrometer using a monochromatized Al Kα radiation. The scanning electron microscope (SEM) was used to characterize the morphologies and size of the synthesized sample. The chemical composition was investigated via energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) measurements were conducted on Hitachi 800 and JEOL-2010 microscope, respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Nicolet 500 infrared spectrometer in the range 4000–400cm−1 using pressed KBr discs. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were performed on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer equipped with an integrating sphere assembly with BaSO4 as the reflectance standard and with the range of 200–800nm. The UV–vis absorption spectra were collected on a UV-2800 spectrophotometer.

The photocatalytic activities of NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites were evaluated by the photocatalytic degradation of Methylene blue (MB) under visible light irradiation. A 42W LED panel (giving wavelength range of 420–700nm, Huerler, China) was used for visible light irradiation. The light source was positioned 15cm above the surface of reaction solution. Aqueous solution of MB (100mL, 10mgL−1) were added in the different Pyrex photocatalytic reactors. In every experiment, 0.1g NCQDs/TiO2 composites were suspended into pollutant aqueous solution and stirred for 12h to achieve the adsorption equilibrium in dark. Ca. 4mL of suspension was taken out every 60min and centrifuged at 8000rpm for 5min to remove the photocatalyst particles. The concentrations of MB were determined by an UV–vis spectrophotometer according to its absorbance at 664nm.

Fig. 1 shows the different optical properties between CQDs and NCQDs by the UV–vis absorption spectrum. Compared to the CQDs, the NCQDs have a broad UV–vis absorption below 400 nm. Two characteristic peaks centered at around 240 and 350 nm. The adsorption peak at 240 nm, which was conform to other reports about carbon quantum dots, may be attributable to π-π* electronic transitions of the aromatic sp2 domains and the n-π* transition of CO bond [28,31]. The peak centered at 350nm can be ascribed to the typical adsorption pattern of carbon nitrides semiconductor, which was similar to other reported NCQDs [28,32].

Insets shows that the CQDs solution was transparent and clear while NCQDs solution exhibited strong blue fluorescence under UV excitation from a 365nm lamp. This is similar to the graphene quantum dots and probably attributable to their small size and surface states [28,31]. According to recent achievements, surface amino group process was only changed the surface groups of the CQDs, but there was no change about the structure of the CQDs [28,32,33]. With the reduction of the non-radiation recombination center, the fluorescence intensity of the carbon quantum dots was enhanced. The result of UV–vis absorption spectrum could demonstrate the successful preparation of NCQDs. Compared to the CQDs, the NCQDs shows absorption in a broad spectrum range, this behavior is contributed to better optical properties than CQDs.

As shown in Fig. 2 , the phase structures of the as-prepared TiO2 and NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites were examined by XRD. All TiO2 and NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites exhibited the same characteristic diffraction peaks of anatase TiO2 (JCPDS card No. 21-1272). The doping of NCQDs into the reaction system has no obvious influence on the crystallinity and phase purity of the resultant products. Moreover, no signal about NCQDs can be detected, which is explained by the small amounts, high dispersion and low crystallinity of NCQDs in NCQDs/TiO2 composites. The result can also be found in similar systems [9].

The XPS spectrum of NCQDs/TiO2 were shown in Fig. 3 . The binding energies at 284.6, 458.0 and 529.1eV for characteristic peaks are corresponding to C 1s, Ti 2p and O 1s, respectively (Fig. 3a). Two peaks at 458.0 and 463.7eV in the Ti 2p spectrum are corresponding to the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 of TiO2, respectively. No peak corresponding to Ti3+ is observed in the Ti 2p3/2 core level spectra (Fig. 3b), indicating the absence of chemical environment of Ti3+. This suggests that the Ti4+ is the main form of Ti in NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites. There are 3 diffraction peaks in C 1s spectrum, which are 284.6eV, 285.3eV and 288.4eV, respectively. The main peak of C 1s spectrum at 284.6eV is attributed to the CC bond with sp2 orbital. The peak at 285.3 and 288.4eV are assigned to the CO/CN and CO/CN bonds, respectively [20]. No peak of C-Ti bond is found in both Ti 2p and C 1s spectra, which indicates that NCQDs do not exist as a dopant in TiO2. The O 1s spectrum fitted to four peaks at 529.1, 529.7, 531.3 and 532.7eV are ascribed to Ti-O, CO and OH, respectively [34,35]. Both the C 1s and O 1s spectra indicate the existence of CO/CO bonds in NCQDs/TiO2 composites. Four peaks at 397.7, 398.9, 399.3 and 400.3eV for N 1s spectrum are assigned to the CN, CN and NH bonds [28].

The morphology and microstructure of the as-obtained NCQDs/TiO2 were characterized by SEM and EDS analysis (Fig. 4 ). The entire hierarchical structures consist of numerous NCQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles. From the SEM images, the surface of NCQDs/TiO2 is obviously rough. This demonstrated that the NCQDs may be mainly deposited on the octahedral TiO2 surface. NCQDs are difficult to discern through SEM due to their very small size. The EDS pattern shows the NCQDs/TiO2 sample contain C, N, Ti and O element. The compositional analysis shows the elemental contents of C, N, Ti and O in inset Table of Fig. 4b. This is consistent with the results of XPS spectra analysis.

It can be observed that a large number of NCQDs are evenly attached to the surface of TiO2 (Fig. 4c), which is a strong evidence to explain the formation of NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites. Fig. 4d shows the HRTEM image of NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites. The lattice spacing of NCQDs is determined to be 0.321nm, which corresponding to the (002) crystal plane [23,36]. The lattice fringe spacing of some NCQDs cannot be observed and this is due to that NCQDs are quasi-spherical nanoparticles comprising amorphous to nanocrystalline cores [28]. At the same time, the lattice spacing around 0.350nm can be found, which agree well with the crystallographic (101) spacing of TiO2. The above results further demonstrate that the NCQDs have modified on the TiO2 to construct NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites.

The FT-IR spectra were further used to determine the presence of NCQDs in NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites. And the existing interactions between NCQDs and TiO2 can be further confirmed by FT-IR analysis. As shown in Fig. 5 , the stretching vibrations of OH at 3439cm−1 and the NH at 3392cm−1 as well as the CH at 2972cm−1 can be observed. The peak at 1404cm−1 of CO stretching vibration for NCQDs shifted to 1645cm−1 for NCQDs/TiO2 composites and a new peak at 1384cm−1 of epoxy CO stretching vibration appeared in NCQDs/TiO2 composites [9,20]. The absorption bands at 1635cm−1 are associated with stretching vibrations of carboxylate group. This indicates that some functional groups containing N on the surfaces of NCQDs were converted into carboxylic functional group. A red shift of the peaks of carboxyl was observed, which indicated that the surface complexation of TiO2 with the carboxylic functional group derived from the surfaces of NCQDs occurs[9,21]. The result of FT-IR analysis is consistent with the XPS analysis.

In order to study the functions of NCQDs, the light absorption property of pristine TiO2 and the NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites with different NCQDs contents were analyzed via UV–vis DRS. As shown in Fig. 6 , pristine TiO2 exhibits no absorption in the region of 400–800nm, which accords with the intrinsic absorption property of TiO2. In addition, 0.2NCQDs/TiO2 and 10NCQDs/TiO2 shows strong absorption in the UV region, while shows no absorption in the visible region. Other NCQDs/TiO2 composites exhibit obvious absorption in visible region ranging besides the strong UV absorption. It can be seen that the absorption intensity of NCQDs/TiO2 composites increase with increasing the amount of NCQDs in a certain range. However, according to other researchers [37], over dose of the NCQDs on the surface of TiO2 tend to aggregate together to form lager particles (>10nm), resulting in weak visible light absorption by the NCQDs/TiO2. This is the possible reason that 10NCQDs/TiO2 possesses lower absorption intensity in visible light. No typical absorption at λ>600nm induced by Ti3+ ions was found [38], suggesting that visible light absorption of NCQDs/TiO2 was ascribed to the introduction of NCQDs. This might help to improve the photocatalytic activity under the visible light irradiation.

Fig. 7 a shows the photocatalytic activities of the NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites with different NCQDs contents as well as pristine TiO2 under the visible light irradiation. The control tests shown no obvious MB removal by either light irradiation or catalyst adsorption. It can be seen that the introduction of different contents of NCQDs could significantly enhance the photocatalytic performance of TiO2. The photodegradation efficiency of MB over 1NCQDs/TiO2 was more than 86.9% in 420min under visible light irradiation, which was much higher than those for pristine TiO2 and the hybrid composites with other NCQDs contents (below 80.1%). The pristine TiO2 showed slow photocatalytic degradation of MB with a final MB removal efficiency of 53.8% at 420min. This kind of phenomenon can be explained by the dye photosensitization mechanism [39,40]. The 10 NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites exhibited the lowest photodegradation rate (49.3% removal efficiency), in that the excessive NCQDs reduces the adsorption of MB on TiO2 surface. In addition, as a representative photocatalyst, an examination to the recycled photocatalytic activity of 1NCQDs/TiO2 was also carried out, and the corresponding result was presented in Fig. 7b. In the four cyclic MB tests, the 1NCQDs/TiO2 exhibited an excellent stability with an average MB removal efficiency, and no evident change has taken place for the photocatalytic activity, which indicates good photocatalytic stability of the NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites.

Based on the above experimental results, the photocatalytic mechanism diagram of NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites is proposed and illustrated in Fig. 8 . Firstly, the dye molecules are adsorbed onto the NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites surface where the photocatalytic reaction takes place. When the visible light is absorbed by MB dye molecules, it was stimulated to produce excited states (MB*). The electrons can be excited from the MB* to the conductive band (CB) of TiO2 and the MB* transforms into MB+•, which is essential to further photocatalytic reaction. The energy level of the excited MB* was located slightly above the CB of TiO2, which providing a possible driving force for electron injection. Normally, these electrons quickly recombine with other ions and only a portion of charges could participate in the photocatalytic process. However, when TiO2 is contact with NCQDs to form NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites, these electrons on the CB of TiO2 tend to transfer to NCQDs. The NCQDs made it easier to transfer the electrons, result in efficient electron-MB+• pair separation and thus an enhanced photocatalytic activity. The electron on the NCQDs could combine with oxygen in the solution to form O2 −. The generated O2 − would play important role in the photodegradation process, leading to dramatic photocatalytic performance. As a result, the obviously improved visible photocatalytic activity of NCQDs/TiO2 should be attributed to the function of NCQDs, not only the sensitization effect of MB to TiO2.

The NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites were prepared via a simple and efficient low-temperature process. The results characterized by various techniques revealed that NCQDs were successfully doped onto the TiO2 surface. Compared with pristine TiO2, the introduction of NCQDs onto TiO2 surface could significantly increase the adsorption of visible light. The NCQDs played important roles of excellent electron transfer/reservoir for MB degradation under visible light irradiation. The 1NCQDs/TiO2 hybrid composites exhibited the highest photocatalytic performance for MB removal. On the basis of the MB dye photosensitization, the NCQDs could greatly improve charge separation efficiency.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors greatly thank the State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology (QA201418), the Science and Technology Development Program of Jilin province, China (20140101159JC) and Graduate Innovation Fund of Jilin University (2015057) for financial support.
